title: How Do Student Loans Actually Work? (Interest Explained Simply)
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You sign the paperwork. You get money for college. You graduate. Then the bills start. The amount you owe is bigger than you remember. This is how student loans work. Here is what you need to understand before you borrow.

The Basic Math of Borrowing

You borrow money. The lender charges you for the privilege. That charge is interest. Interest is calculated as a percentage of what you owe. The higher the percentage, the more you pay.

Simple example: You borrow $10,000 at 5% interest per year. After one year, you owe $10,500. The extra $500 is interest. If you don't pay it, that interest gets added to what you owe. Next year, you pay interest on $10,500, not $10,000. This is compound interest.

Compound interest is powerful when you're saving. It's painful when you're borrowing.

Subsidized vs. Unsubsidized Loans

Subsidized loans: The government pays the interest while you're in school. You don't owe interest during your grace period. This is the best kind of loan. They are only available to students with financial need.

Unsubsidized loans: Interest starts accruing immediately. Even while you're in school. Even during your grace period. That interest gets added to your balance. By the time you graduate, you owe more than you borrowed.

If you have a choice, take subsidized loans first. They are cheaper.

The True Cost of Borrowing

Let's do real math. You borrow $30,000 at 5% interest over 10 years. Your monthly payment: about $318. Total paid: $38,183. You pay $8,183 in interest.

Same loan over 20 years: monthly payment about $198. Total paid: $47,520. You pay $17,520 in interest.

Lower monthly payments feel better now. They cost much more later. Pay as much as you can, as early as you can.

The Grace Period

After graduation, you have a grace period (usually 6 months) before payments start. Interest may or may not accrue during this time, depending on your loan type. Use this time to prepare. Set up autopay. Understand your budget.

If you can start paying during the grace period, do it. Even small payments reduce your total interest.

Income-Driven Repayment Plans

If your income is low after graduation, you can enroll in an income-driven repayment plan. Your payment is based on your income, not your loan balance. You might pay $0 per month. After 20-25 years, the remaining balance is forgiven.

Forgiven debt is taxable as income. You'll owe taxes on the forgiven amount. Plan for this.

Income-driven plans extend your repayment term. You pay more interest overall. But they provide breathing room when you need it.

Loan Forgiveness Programs

Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF): Work for a government or nonprofit organization for 10 years. Make 120 qualifying payments. Your remaining balance is forgiven tax-free.

This is a great deal if you plan to work in public service. But the rules are strict. Make sure you understand them. Many applicants get rejected because they had the wrong loan type or payment plan.

Teacher loan forgiveness: Up to $17,500 forgiven after 5 years in a low-income school. Also available.

What to Do Before You Borrow

Calculate your future monthly payment. Use a student loan calculator. Multiply that by 120 (10 years). Is that number comfortable? Is it terrifying? Adjust your borrowing accordingly.

Remember the rule of thumb: Don't borrow more than your expected first-year salary. If you expect to earn $50,000 your first year out of college, don't borrow more than $50,000 total.

Student loans are not inherently bad. They are a tool. Used wisely, they enable education that increases your earning potential. Used unwisely, they burden you for decades. Borrow what you need. Borrow as little as possible. Understand the terms before you sign.
